sequence_meta.persona_voice: Emily Weiss / Into The Gloss — community voice, skin-type aware, friend-to-friend, personal stories, never brand-to-consumer

emails.subject_line: Your free skincare guide + a question about your skin
emails.preview_text: I want to understand what you're actually dealing with before I recommend anything
emails.plain_text_body:
Hey, welcome.

I'm Chloe. I've been writing about skincare for about six years — not from a brand perspective, from a person-who-cares-too-much-about-this perspective. I've tried most of what I write about. I've made a lot of mistakes. I've wasted money on things that didn't work and found incredible things in places I didn't expect.

Your guide — "The Edited Routine: A Skin-Type Specific Starter Stack for Every Budget" — is attached. Before you dig in, though, I want to ask you something because the guide covers four different skin types and I don't want you to read all four sections if you only need one.

What does your skin actually do?

Click the option that's most true, and I'll make sure everything I send from here on out is relevant to what you're actually dealing with:

[SEGMENTATION LINK: Oily/Combination] — "My T-zone is shiny by noon and my pores look like craters close-up"
[SEGMENTATION LINK: Dry/Sensitive] — "My skin gets tight, flaky, or red — especially in winter"
[SEGMENTATION LINK: Acne-Prone] — "I break out regularly and nothing seems to stop it permanently"
[SEGMENTATION LINK: Hyperpigmentation] — "My main concern is dark spots, uneven tone, or post-acne marks"
[SEGMENTATION LINK: Anti-Aging] — "I'm focused on fine lines, texture, and keeping what I have"

No wrong answers. These are all just different problems with different solutions.

The thing I want you to know before anything else: building a skincare routine is not complicated. The skincare industry makes it complicated because complicated sells more products. A genuinely effective routine for most people is three to four steps.

Cleanse. Treat. Moisturize. SPF in the morning.

Every other product fits inside one of those steps or adds a fifth one if your skin needs it.

I'm going to send you emails that actually help you figure out which products belong in your routine for your skin — not the ones that are trending on TikTok, not the ones with the nicest packaging, the ones that work.

Talk soon,
Chloe
The Skin Edit at Mintbrooks

P.S. — I share affiliate links in most emails. I only ever share things I've used, researched thoroughly, or seen consistent community praise for. I'll tell you what I use myself vs. what I've researched when it matters.
emails.html_body:
The Skin Edit · Mintbrooks

Your free skincare guide — and one question

Hey, welcome. I'm Chloe.

Before you read the guide, I want to know what your skin actually does — so everything I send is relevant to you.

What's most true for your skin?

  • Oily/Combination — shiny T-zone, visible pores
  • Dry/Sensitive — tight, flaky, or red
  • Acne-prone — breaks out regularly
  • Hyperpigmentation — dark spots, uneven tone
  • Anti-aging — fine lines, texture, prevention

A genuinely effective routine is 3-4 steps. Cleanse. Treat. Moisturize. SPF. I'll help you fill those steps with what actually works for your skin.

Talk soon,
Chloe

emails.subject_line: The skincare ingredient I avoided for years (and now can't live without)
emails.preview_text: I thought retinol was for my mom. I was so wrong.
emails.plain_text_body:
Okay, confession.

I avoided retinol for three years because I thought it was a "serious" thing that only dermatologists prescribed and that it would make my skin peel off and become red and sensitive forever.

This is what happens when you get your skincare education from beauty forums.

Retinol is Vitamin A. Your skin has receptors specifically designed to use it. It works by accelerating cell turnover — basically telling your skin cells to move faster, which means new, fresh cells surface more quickly and dead skin sheds faster.

The result over 12-16 weeks of consistent use: smoother texture, smaller pores (they look smaller when they're not clogged), faded hyperpigmentation, and — this is the big one — stimulation of collagen production.

It's the closest thing to an anti-aging ingredient with real, robust evidence behind it. More than any serum. More than most professional treatments.

The reason people hate it is that they start wrong. They buy prescription-strength tretinoin or a high-percentage OTC retinol and use it every night and their skin freaks out. This is called retinization — it's real, it's temporary, and it's completely preventable.

The right way to start:

1. Start with a low-percentage retinol (0.025% to 0.05%)
2. Use it two nights a week for the first month
3. Increase to three nights a week in month two
4. By month three, every other night is fine for most people
5. Always follow with a moisturizer. Always use SPF the next morning.

The products I'd actually recommend for starting:

**For complete beginners or sensitive skin:**
Olay Regenerist Retinol 24 Night Moisturizer — retinol plus hyaluronic acid, so it delivers moisture at the same time. Much gentler entry point. About $30.

**For people ready for a proper retinol serum:**
Paula's Choice 1% Retinol Treatment — this is what I use. Excellent formulation, well-tolerated by most people who've eased in properly. About $52.

**If you want pharmacy-level without the price:**
Adaptalene (Differin Gel, 0.1%) — this is a retinoid that was previously prescription only and is now available OTC. Particularly good for acne-prone skin. About $14.

[PRODUCT LINK: Olay Regenerist Retinol 24 Night Moisturizer]
[PRODUCT LINK: Paula's Choice 1% Retinol Treatment]
[PRODUCT LINK: Differin Adapalene Gel 0.1% Acne Treatment]

One thing I want to be clear about: if you're pregnant or breastfeeding, you should skip retinoids entirely. Talk to your OB. The rest of us — this is worth starting.

Next email: the SPF conversation. Yes, we're having it. And I'm going to change your mind about the kind you're using.

Talk soon,
Chloe

P.S. — The patch test rule is not optional. New product, new ingredient — patch test on your jaw or inner arm for 48 hours before putting it on your full face. Always.
emails.html_body:
The Skin Edit · Mintbrooks

The ingredient I avoided for 3 years

I avoided retinol because I thought it was serious and scary. It's not. Here's what I wish I'd known.

Retinol is Vitamin A. Your skin has receptors designed to use it. It accelerates cell turnover — new cells surface faster, pores look smaller, hyperpigmentation fades, and collagen production is stimulated.

It's the closest thing to an anti-aging ingredient with real evidence behind it.

How to start without the freak-out:

  1. Start at 0.025%–0.05%
  2. Use 2 nights a week, first month
  3. Increase to 3 nights in month two
  4. Always follow with moisturizer
  5. Always use SPF the next morning

My recommendations by starting point:

  • Olay Regenerist Retinol 24 Night Moisturizer
    ~$30 — For complete beginners or sensitive skin. Retinol + hyaluronic acid.
  • Paula's Choice 1% Retinol Treatment
    ~$52 — What I personally use. For people ready for a proper retinol serum.
  • Differin Adapalene Gel 0.1%
    ~$14 — Previously prescription-only retinoid. Great for acne-prone skin.

Talk soon,
Chloe

Patch test always. Avoid if pregnant/breastfeeding.

emails.subject_line: "My skin has never looked better" (and she's 47)
emails.preview_text: A reader's story about finding the routine that actually stuck
emails.plain_text_body:
I got this message a few weeks ago and I've been thinking about it constantly.

Sharing with permission from Rachel, who reads this newsletter from Vancouver:

---

"I've been battling with my skin since I had my first kid at 32. The hormonal acne came with pregnancy and it just... never fully left. I've spent probably thousands of dollars over 15 years trying things that didn't work.

Last year I started reading your emails. I know that sounds dramatic, but the thing that shifted for me was when you explained that not all dark spots are the same — some are hyperpigmentation from inflammation, some are melasma from sun/hormones, and they respond to completely different ingredients.

I'd been using Vitamin C on what turned out to be melasma-adjacent spots, which was doing essentially nothing.

I switched to Tranexamic Acid (the Naturium one you mentioned) and added niacinamide. Within about 8 weeks I saw more progress than I'd seen in two years.

I'm 47. My skin has never looked better. I cried a little bit, honestly."

— Rachel, Vancouver

---

Rachel said something that I think about a lot: that the problem wasn't effort. She'd been trying for 15 years. The problem was targeting the wrong mechanism.

This is the thing that separates skincare that works from skincare that doesn't. It's not about how much you spend or how many steps you do. It's about identifying what your skin is actually doing and matching the ingredient to the mechanism.

Hyperpigmentation from inflammation (PIH — post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation): responds to niacinamide, azelaic acid, and sometimes retinol.

Melasma (hormonally-driven pigmentation): responds to tranexamic acid, kojic acid, and strict sun protection. Vitamin C helps, but it's not first-line.

Sun spots (lentigines): respond to retinoids, acids, and brighteners. These are the most treatable.

The products Rachel used:

[PRODUCT LINK: Naturium Tranexamic Acid Serum 5%]
[PRODUCT LINK: The Ordinary Niacinamide 10% + Zinc 1%]

If you're not sure which type you're dealing with — a good dermatologist can tell you in about 30 seconds under a light. It's worth one appointment if you've been frustrated for a long time.

But for most people reading this? The combination of niacinamide + tranexamic acid is a good place to start for any discoloration concern. Both are gentle. Both have strong evidence. And the Ordinary niacinamide is $7.

Talk soon,
Chloe

P.S. — If you have a story like Rachel's — a moment when things clicked and your routine finally started working — I would genuinely love to hear it. Hit reply.

emails.subject_line: The SPF you've been using is probably not doing what you think
emails.preview_text: Chemical vs. mineral, reapplication reality, and the one I actually use
emails.plain_text_body:
We need to talk about sunscreen.

I know. Bear with me.

Here's the thing most beauty content won't say plainly: all the retinol, niacinamide, vitamin C, and expensive serums in the world are doing half the job if you're not wearing SPF every morning. Sunscreen is the single most evidence-backed anti-aging and anti-pigmentation tool that exists. More than any serum. More than injectables.

UV exposure is responsible for about 80% of visible facial aging. That's a well-studied number.

So why do most people skip it? Because the experience of wearing SPF has historically been terrible. White cast. Greasy finish. Pilling under makeup. The smell of a 1990s beach.

This is changing. And quickly.

A few things I want you to know:

**Chemical vs. mineral sunscreen — the real difference:**
Mineral (zinc oxide and/or titanium dioxide): sits on the skin and reflects UV rays. Immediately effective. Often leaves a white cast on deeper skin tones. Better for sensitive skin and reef-safe concerns.
Chemical (avobenzone, octinoxate, etc.): absorbs into the skin and converts UV to heat. No white cast. Needs 20 minutes to activate. There are some concerns about hormone disruption at high doses, though the FDA is still reviewing this.

Neither is universally better. Mineral with a good tint or formulation is often the best of both.

**The reapplication reality nobody talks about:**
SPF protection degrades. In direct sun, you need to reapply every 2 hours. Most people apply once in the morning and consider themselves protected all day.

For most of us working indoors, once in the morning is probably fine (UVA comes through windows, but at much lower intensity). But if you're outside? Reapply.

The products I use and love:

**My everyday SPF (7 years running):**
EltaMD UV Clear Broad-Spectrum SPF 46 — tinted or untinted, absorbs beautifully, doesn't pill, has niacinamide built in. About $40. Worth every dollar.

**For deeper skin tones (no white cast):**
Black Girl Sunscreen SPF 30 — no white cast, no scent, moisturizing finish. Dermatologist community favorite.

**Budget-friendly option that actually works:**
La Roche-Posay Anthelios Melt-in Milk SPF 100 — yes, SPF 100 is overkill in theory but real-world application means you get SPF 30-40 effective protection. This one passes the vibe test.

**For reapplication over makeup:**
Supergoop Invisible Mist SPF 40 — spray on top of makeup. No rubbing. Refreshes protection without disturbing your look.

[PRODUCT LINK: EltaMD UV Clear SPF 46 Tinted]
[PRODUCT LINK: Black Girl Sunscreen SPF 30]
[PRODUCT LINK: La Roche-Posay Anthelios Melt-in Milk SPF 100]
[PRODUCT LINK: Supergoop! Invisible Setting Mist SPF 40]

The one non-negotiable: broad-spectrum. This is printed on the bottle. It means the formula covers both UVA (aging) and UVB (burning). If it doesn't say broad-spectrum, don't bother.

Talk soon,
Chloe

P.S. — Your neck and chest are part of your face. They see the same sun. Please include them.

Chemical vs. Mineral: Neither is universally better. Mineral = reflects UV, no chemical concerns, sometimes white cast. Chemical = no white cast, needs 20min to activate, some formulation concerns at high doses.

The non-negotiable: Broad-spectrum. Printed on the bottle. Both UVA + UVB covered.

My picks by use case:

  • EltaMD UV Clear SPF 46 Tinted
    My everyday for 7 years. Absorbs perfectly, has niacinamide, doesn't pill.
  • Black Girl Sunscreen SPF 30
    No white cast, no scent. For deeper skin tones.

    Budget-friendly, excellent real-world protection.
  • Supergoop! Invisible Setting Mist SPF 40

Talk soon,
Chloe

emails.subject_line: Your skin type routine (the short version)
emails.preview_text: Oily, dry, acne-prone, or hyperpigmentation — here's your specific stack
emails.plain_text_body:
Three weeks ago you told me about your skin. Today I'm giving you back a specific routine for it.

Scroll to your skin type. The rest is background noise.

---

**IF YOUR SKIN IS: Oily / Combination**

Your goal: control shine without stripping, keep pores clear, prevent the congestion that leads to breakouts.

AM Routine:
1. Cleanser: CeraVe Foaming Facial Cleanser — removes excess oil without disrupting barrier
2. Niacinamide serum: The Ordinary Niacinamide 10% + Zinc — regulates sebum production
3. Lightweight moisturizer: Neutrogena Hydro Boost Water Gel — hydration without heaviness
4. SPF: EltaMD UV Clear SPF 46 (the one in this formula controls shine too)

PM Routine:
1. Same cleanser or oil cleanser if wearing makeup
2. Retinol 2x/week (Differin Gel 0.1% or Olay Regenerist)
3. Same moisturizer

---

**IF YOUR SKIN IS: Dry / Sensitive**

Your goal: rebuild and maintain the skin barrier. Barrier-compromised skin is the root of most dry skin issues.

AM Routine:
1. Cleanser: CeraVe Hydrating Cleanser — milk-based, doesn't strip anything
2. Hyaluronic acid serum: Neutrogena Hydro Boost + Vitamin C (apply to damp skin for maximum humectant effect)
3. Moisturizer: CeraVe Moisturizing Cream (not lotion — the cream in the tub) — ceramides rebuild barrier
4. SPF: EltaMD UV Physical Broad-Spectrum SPF 41 (mineral, no chemical irritants)

PM Routine:
1. Oil cleanser + water cleanser if wearing makeup
2. Moisturizer: First Aid Beauty Ultra Repair Cream or Vanicream Moisturizing Skin Cream
3. Occasional face oil on dry patches: Biossance Squalane Oil (squalane is identical to your skin's natural sebum)

---

**IF YOUR SKIN IS: Acne-Prone**

Your goal: prevent new breakouts, accelerate healing, prevent PIH.

AM Routine:
1. Cleanser: La Roche-Posay Toleriane Purifying Foaming Cleanser
2. Azelaic Acid: The Ordinary Azelaic Acid Suspension 10% — anti-inflammatory, unclogs pores
3. Lightweight moisturizer: CeraVe AM Facial Moisturizing Lotion with SPF (two steps in one)

PM Routine:
1. Same cleanser
2. Adapalene (Differin 0.1%) — begin 2x/week, increase slowly
3. Moisturizer
4. Spot treatment as needed: Benzac 2.5% benzoyl peroxide (low dose is less irritating, equally effective)

---

**IF YOUR CONCERN IS: Hyperpigmentation / Dark Spots**

Your goal: fade existing spots, prevent new ones, maintain overall tone.

AM Routine:
1. Cleanser
2. Vitamin C serum: TruSkin Vitamin C Serum — L-ascorbic acid 20%, brightening + antioxidant protection
3. Moisturizer
4. SPF: Non-negotiable. Pigmentation gets worse without it.

PM Routine:
1. Cleanser
2. Tranexamic Acid: Naturium Tranexamic Acid Serum 5% — fades existing pigmentation
3. Niacinamide: The Ordinary 10% + Zinc
4. Moisturizer + retinol 2-3x/week

---

All of these routines are designed to be affordable, evidence-backed, and starting-point focused. None of them require spending more than $80-100 total to start.

[PRODUCT LINK: CeraVe Foaming Facial Cleanser 16 oz]
[PRODUCT LINK: CeraVe Moisturizing Cream 16 oz tub]
[PRODUCT LINK: TruSkin Vitamin C Serum for face]
[PRODUCT LINK: Naturium Tranexamic Acid Serum 5%]

Talk soon,
Chloe

emails.preview_text: A month in — I want to hear from you
emails.plain_text_body:
A month ago you downloaded a guide and told me about your skin.

I'm checking in.

Because I know how this goes. You open the emails, you learn things, maybe you order one product, maybe you're still meaning to but haven't yet. Life is busy. Skincare feels lower-priority than approximately everything else.

I get it.

But here's what I want to say: the gap between the routine you have and the one that would actually make a difference is smaller than you think. Most people I talk to are 1-2 products away from a routine that changes how they feel about their skin.

Not a 10-step overhaul. One addition or one swap.

I want to help you find that one thing. So I'm going to ask:

What's the thing you're most unhappy about with your skin right now? Not your whole routine, not your whole face — the one thing that bothers you most when you look in the mirror.

Hit reply and tell me. I'll respond personally to as many as I can.

In the meantime, I'll keep sending you content every week or so — ingredient deep dives, reviews, the occasional find I can't stop thinking about.

If you're not feeling it anymore, the unsubscribe is below. Truly no pressure.

But if you're still working on your routine — welcome to the ongoing project. Skin takes time. It's worth it.

Talk soon,
Chloe

P.S. — One thing I keep coming back to for almost every skin concern: moisturizer matters more than most people think. If you haven't tried switching to a ceramide-based cream, this is the version I'd start with:

[PRODUCT LINK: CeraVe Moisturizing Cream 16 oz tub — the one that dermatologists won't shut up about]

It's $18. It genuinely works. It's not exciting. That's kind of the point.
